chunk_summary: This section discusses condition-specific modifications to iron therapy in severe acute malnutrition (SAM), hemoglobinopathies, malabsorption, and prematurity, emphasizing clinical guidelines to prevent harm and optimize treatment outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the contraindications and timing of iron therapy in SAM during stabilization and rehabilitation phases.
Learn diagnostic approaches for anemia in hemoglobinopathies and transfusion-dependent conditions.
Identify strategies for managing iron deficiency in malabsorption syndromes and chronic inflammation.
Explore iron supplementation protocols for preterm and low birth weight infants.
key_facts: Iron is avoided during the stabilization phase of SAM due to infection risk and metabolic instability; introduced only during rehabilitation after appetite returns.
Transfusion-dependent thalassemia anemia is not iron deficiency; routine prophylactic iron is contraindicated unless deficiency is confirmed.
Persistent microcytosis despite therapy warrants evaluation for conditions like celiac disease, chronic diarrhea, or CKD; ferritin interpretation should account for inflammation.
Preterm infants often require early iron supplementation at approximately 2 mg/kg/day elemental iron starting in early infancy.
Adherence barriers to oral iron include vomiting, refusal, and stool discoloration; these must be addressed before escalating doses.
reference_quote: In facility-based management of complicated SAM, iron is avoided during the stabilization phase because infection risk and metabolic instability are highest. (NHM Operational Guidelines)
document_key_topics: Iron therapy in severe acute malnutrition (SAM)
Iron supplementation in malabsorption and chronic inflammation
Prematurity and low birth weight iron protocols
